magnets.title: Magnets
keyQuestions: What are the two poles of a magnet?
What happens when like poles meet? Unlike poles?
What materials are attracted to magnets?
Is every iron object a magnet?
Where is a magnet's force the strongest?
keyIdeas: Magnetic force is the force exerted between a magnet and a magnetic material, or between two magnets. It can act at a distance.
All magnets have two poles: a north pole and a south pole. Like poles repel each other; unlike poles attract.
Only iron, steel, nickel, and cobalt are magnetic materials. Other metals like aluminium, copper, and gold are not magnetic.
The magnetic force is strongest at the poles and weakest at the middle of a magnet. Magnetic force can act at a distance.
The Earth behaves like a giant magnet. A compass needle is magnetised so its north end points to geographic north, which is near Earth's magnetic south pole.

glossary.definition: An object that has the property of attracting magnetic materials. All magnets have a north and a south pole.
The two ends of a magnet where its magnetic force is strongest. Called the north (N) and south (S) pole.
The force pulling two unlike poles (N–S) together. Unlike poles attract.
The force pushing two like poles (N–N or S–S) apart. Like poles repel.
A material attracted to a magnet. E.g. iron, steel, nickel, cobalt. NOT all metals — aluminium, copper, gold are not magnetic.
A material not attracted to a magnet. E.g. wood, plastic, glass, aluminium, copper, rubber.
A magnet that keeps its magnetism. E.g. bar magnet, horseshoe magnet, button magnet.
A device with a magnetised needle that always points toward geographic north. Used for navigation.

habitats.title: Habitats & Food Chains
keyQuestions: What is a habitat?
What is a food chain?
What is a producer? A consumer? A decomposer?
How does energy flow along a food chain?
What is a food web?
What happens if one organism is removed from a food web?
keyIdeas: A food chain shows how energy is passed from one living thing to another, starting with a producer (usually a green plant) and moving to consumers.
A food web is made up of many interconnected food chains in the same habitat. A change to one organism can affect many others.
A habitat is the natural home of an organism, providing food, shelter, water, and space. Different habitats support different living things.
Producers (green plants) make their own food using sunlight. Consumers eat other living things. Decomposers break down dead matter and return nutrients to the soil.

glossary.definition: The natural environment where an organism lives, finds food, shelter, and mates. Examples: forest, ocean, desert, pond, rainforest.
Any living thing — plants, animals, fungi, and bacteria are all organisms.
A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to the next through feeding. Always starts with a producer.
glossary.template: A sequence that shows how ___ is passed from ___ to ___.

glossary.definition: A network of many interconnected food chains in an ecosystem. More realistic than a single food chain.
An organism that makes its own food using sunlight (photosynthesis). Always a plant or plant-like organism. E.g. grass, trees, algae.
glossary.template: An organism that makes its own food by ___.

glossary.definition: An organism that eats producers (plants). Also called a herbivore. E.g. rabbit, caterpillar, grasshopper, deer.
An organism that eats primary consumers. Can be a carnivore or omnivore. E.g. frog, fox, small snake.
An animal that hunts and eats other animals. E.g. hawk, snake, fox, frog.
An animal that is hunted and eaten by a predator. E.g. rabbit, grasshopper, caterpillar.
An organism that breaks down dead plants and animals, returning nutrients to the soil. E.g. mushrooms (fungi), bacteria, worms.
A feature that helps an organism survive in its habitat. E.g. a camel's hump stores fat for energy in the desert.
glossary.template: A ___ of an organism that helps it to ___.

glossary.definition: The surroundings of an organism, including all living and non-living things around it.

forces.title: Forces
keyQuestions: What is a force?
How does friction affect a moving object?
What factors affect the amount of friction?
How does gravitational force change with mass?
What is Hooke's Law?
What is the resultant force and why does it matter?
keyIdeas: A force is a push or a pull. A force can change the shape, speed, or direction of an object, and can start or stop motion.
Gravitational force is the force that pulls objects towards the centre of the Earth. Weight is the gravitational force acting on an object.

Frictional force is a force that opposes motion between two surfaces in contact. It acts in the opposite direction to motion and depends on the roughness of the surfaces and the weight pressing them together.
Elastic spring force is the force produced by a stretched or compressed elastic object, acting to return the object to its original shape.
In any situation, several forces may act on an object at the same time. The overall effect depends on the size and direction of each force.
The overall motion of an object depends on the resultant force. Equal and opposite forces cancel out; unequal forces change the object's speed or direction.

glossary.definition: A push or pull acting on an object. Forces can change the shape, speed, or direction of an object. Measured in newtons (N).
glossary.template: A ___ or ___ that can change the ___, ___, or ___ of an object.

glossary.definition: A contact force that opposes motion between two surfaces. Acts in the opposite direction to movement. Can be useful (braking) or unhelpful (wear and heat).
The pull of the Earth (or any large mass) on objects. Always acts downward toward the centre of the Earth. Depends on the mass of the object.
glossary.template: The force that pulls objects towards ___.

glossary.definition: The gravitational force acting on an object. Weight = mass × gravitational field strength. Measured in newtons (N), NOT kilograms.
The amount of matter in an object. Measured in kilograms (kg). Mass does NOT change with location; weight does.
The force exerted by a compressed or stretched spring. Follows Hooke's Law: force is proportional to extension (within elastic limit).
The single force that represents the combined effect of all forces on an object. If resultant = 0 N, the object is stationary or moves at constant speed.
When two or more forces cancel each other out (resultant = 0 N). A stationary object has balanced forces.
The maximum force a spring can take before it is permanently deformed and no longer returns to original shape.
Squeezing or pushing a spring so it becomes shorter than its natural length.
Stretching a spring so it becomes longer than its natural length.
The SI unit of force. 1 newton is approximately the weight of a 100 g apple on Earth.
The turning effect of a force about a pivot. Calculated as: Moment = Force × perpendicular distance from pivot. Measured in newton-metres (N·m).
When an object is balanced (in equilibrium), the total clockwise moment equals the total anticlockwise moment about the pivot.

questions.stem: A ball is kicked up into the air. It eventually slows down, stops, and falls back to the ground. Explain using the forces acting on the ball.
questions.modelAnswer: When the ball is kicked, it moves upward. Gravitational force acts on the ball, pulling it downward towards the Earth. This force slows the ball down as it moves up, until it stops momentarily at its highest point. Gravitational force continues to act, pulling the ball back down to the ground.

questions.stem: Why is it easier to push a heavy box on a smooth floor than on a rough carpet?
questions.modelAnswer: Frictional force opposes the motion of the box. The rough carpet produces a larger frictional force between the box and the surface than the smooth floor does. Since more force is needed to overcome the larger frictional force of the carpet, it is harder to push the box on the carpet and easier to push it on the smooth floor.

questions.stem: Alexey noticed that the brake pads on his bicycle wear out more quickly when he rides on rough roads than on smooth roads. Explain why.
questions.modelAnswer: When Alexey applies the brakes, frictional force acts between the brake pads and the wheel to slow the bicycle down. On rough roads, the bicycle shakes more and the brakes are used more often or harder, so there is more friction over time. The greater friction wears away the brake pads more quickly.

questions.stem: Explain how a polar bear is adapted to survive in the cold Arctic environment.
questions.modelAnswer: A polar bear has thick fur and a layer of fat under its skin. The thick fur traps a layer of air, which is a poor conductor of heat, reducing heat loss from the body. The layer of fat also acts as an insulator, keeping the bear warm in the cold Arctic. Its white fur also helps it blend in with the snow to catch prey.

questions.stem: A farmer sprays insecticide on his crops to kill caterpillars. The insecticide also kills many other insects. Explain how this could affect the birds in the area.
questions.modelAnswer: Many of the insects in the area are food for the birds. When the insecticide kills the insects, the birds have less food available. Some birds may not have enough to eat, and their population may decrease. Birds that feed their young on insects may not be able to raise them, further reducing the population.

ecosystems.title: Interactions Within Environment
keyQuestions: What is an ecosystem? How is it different from a habitat?
What is a population? A community?
How do predator and prey populations affect each other?
What is adaptation and give two examples.
What happens when an invasive species is introduced?
Describe two ways humans negatively affect ecosystems.
keyIdeas: An adaptation is a feature that helps an organism to survive in its habitat. Adaptations can be structural (body features) or behavioural (actions).
Human activities such as deforestation, pollution, and over-hunting disrupt food chains and damage habitats. Conservation efforts protect species and ecosystems.
An ecosystem is a community of living things and the non-living parts of their environment, interacting with one another.
Predator-prey relationships keep populations in balance. A change in the number of prey affects the number of predators, and vice versa.

glossary.definition: All the living organisms (biotic factors) and non-living components (abiotic factors: light, water, temperature, soil) in an area, and how they interact.
All the individuals of the SAME species living in the same area at the same time. e.g. all the tigers in a forest.
All the different populations of organisms living together in the same area. e.g. all the tigers, deer, trees, and insects in a forest.
The specific environment where an organism lives and finds what it needs (food, water, shelter). e.g. a pond, a rainforest canopy.
An animal that hunts and kills other animals for food. e.g. lion, hawk, spider.
An animal that is hunted and eaten by predators. e.g. zebra, mouse, fly.
A feature (structural or behavioural) that helps an organism survive in its environment. e.g. cactus has thick stem to store water; polar bear has thick fur for insulation.
glossary.template: A ___ of an organism that helps it to ___.

glossary.definition: The struggle between organisms for the same limited resource (food, water, space, light, mates).
The way organisms depend on each other and on their environment for survival. e.g. plants depend on bees for pollination; bees depend on flowers for nectar.
An organism that makes its own food using light energy (photosynthesis). Always plants/algae. The base of every food chain.
glossary.template: An organism that makes its own food by ___.

glossary.definition: An organism that obtains energy by eating other organisms. Primary consumers eat producers; secondary consumers eat primary consumers.
glossary.template: An organism that gets food by ___.

glossary.definition: Organisms (bacteria, fungi) that break down dead organisms and waste into simple substances. Recycle nutrients back into the ecosystem.
